lede: Buddhist Canonical · พระไตรปิฎก › Abhidhamma accounts for 273 catalogued manuscripts. It clusters in Phrae (56% of the corpus for this sub-genre), ahead of Lampang and Nan. Nearly all (70%) are written in Tham Lanna · อักษรธรรมล้านนา script. By support it leans to palm-leaf · ใบลาน (95%) over mulberry paper (saa) · กระดาษสา (4%). Dated witnesses run 1497–1946 CE (220 of 273 carry a date).

authored.title: Buddhist Canonical › Abhidhamma
authored.body_html:
What this is

This is the higher-doctrine basket — the Abhidhammapiṭaka, the systematic, analytical distillation of the teaching into its bare categories of mind and matter. At 273 witnesses it is the smallest of the three canonical piṭakas here, and by some distance the most technical and most scholastic — the corpus of the specialist.

Approached through its commentary

The most telling pattern is which Abhidhamma text the north actually copied. It is not, overwhelmingly, the seven canonical books in the raw — it is the great commentary on them, the Aṭṭhasālinī (Aṭṭhasālinī, Nisai athasalini, Atthasālinī nāma abhidhammasaṅgiṇī), which recurs again and again, often in multiple phuk (fascicles: phuk si, phuk hok). Around it sit the Saṅgaha digests (Nisai sangkaha, Piccalana ālambana saṅgaha) — the Abhidhammatthasaṅgaha, the standard textbook that made the system teachable. The Lanna Abhidhamma is thus a corpus approached through its exegesis and its compendia, not through the primary canon — you reach the higher doctrine by way of the commentary that explains it.

The shape of the collection

Its physical profile is canonical — 95% palm-leaf (260 of 273), 220 dated, spanning 1497–1946 (its oldest witness rivals the sutta's for the earliest in the collection). Two figures mark it as the scholar's basket. First, language: 142 monolingual Pali against 104 Pali-and-Lan Na — the only canonical sub-genre where untranslated Pali is the majority, the signature of a text read by those who needed no vernacular crutch. Second, concentration: Wat Sung Men holds 152 of 273 (56%) — the highest keystone-library share of the three piṭakas, fitting for the most rarefied material.

Notes

  • The commentary/canon split is the obvious pass. Separating the raw seven books from the Aṭṭhasālinī commentary and the Saṅgaha digests would divide a primary canon from the teaching tradition that carried it — and would tie this sub-genre to the nissaya grammar material, which does the same glossing work.
  • Abhidhamma has a liturgical afterlife. The seven books are themselves recited — the satta-pakaraṇa chant for the dead invokes them by name — so a share of this "study" corpus is also performance; check against the liturgy where funeral recitation lives.
  • Not wichaa-priority; the intellectual apex of the canon. None flagged. Its role in the project is to mark the scholastic ceiling — the most demanding thing the copyists preserved — against which the popular and efficacious genres define themselves.

What the bots have noticed

Auto-derived from the catalogue and the curiosity bots — refreshed automatically, not hand-written. Each note links to its finding.

  • The deepest roots in the collection — The ten earliest-dated manuscripts, reaching back to 1471 CE — the far anchor of the single continuous timeline that runs all the way to today's amulet stalls. see the finding →
